citation: IRC §7520; IRC §2702; Walton v. Commissioner, 115 T.C. 589 (2000)
description: Golden dataset for GRAT zeroed-out remainder valuation. Each case verifies (a) the annuity factor matches the closed-form formula, (b) annuity payment is principal/factor, (c) end-of-term remainder matches a year-by-year simulation. Floating-point reference computations were verified independently and rounded to the cent (ROUND_HALF_EVEN).

cases.name: Standard 5-year GRAT, 5.2% 7520 rate, 8% growth, $5M
cases.rationale: Annuity factor (1 - 1.052^-5)/0.052 ≈ 4.30598. Annuity = 5,000,000 / 4.30598 ≈ 1,161,266.22 (cent-quantized). Year-by-year simulation: portfolio grows 8%, annuity paid end-of-year. Final remainder $533,954.86 is the policy lever — passes to beneficiaries gift-tax-free.

cases.name: 10-year GRAT, 5.2% 7520, 8% growth, $5M
cases.rationale: Long-term GRAT; remainder is positive when g > r. Tested for non-failure + non-negative remainder; specific value verified by simulation.

cases.name: GRAT with growth EQUAL to 7520 rate (zero economic remainder)
cases.rationale: When growth equals the §7520 rate, the GRAT exactly zeroes out at term. Edge case demonstrating that GRAT only transfers wealth above r.

cases.name: GRAT with growth BELOW 7520 rate — fails
cases.rationale: Growth < r → GRAT pays out more than the corpus produces; balance goes negative pre-term; remainder = 0. The 'GRAT failure' scenario all advisors should know.

cases.name: Single-year GRAT — degenerate but legal
cases.rationale: 1-year GRAT: annuity factor = 1/1.05 ≈ 0.95238 → wait, that's PV factor. Annuity factor = (1 - 1.05^-1)/0.05 = 0.95238. Annuity = 1,000,000 / 0.95238 = 1,050,000. After 10% growth: 1,100,000. After paying 1,050,000: 50,000 remainder. Perfect arithmetic check.

cases.name: Long-dated 30-year GRAT (cap)
cases.rationale: Maximum permitted term in this implementation. Tests upper-bound validation.
